steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student wants to provide both an explanation of Kepler's first law and a concrete example of it. The notes supply the key facts: (1) the law says orbits are ellipses, (2) the laws apply to moons as well as planets, and (3) Tethys is a moon with an elliptical orbit. The best choice must use the Tethys example and convey that the law extends beyond planets.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Saturn's moon Tethys completes an orbit in 1.89 Earth days on average, a clear example of Kepler's first law...': The orbital period (1.89 days) has nothing to do with Kepler's first law, which is about the shape of orbits, not their duration. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Tethys's orbit of Saturn is elliptical, demonstrating Kepler's first law of planetary motion, which describes the elliptical orbits of planets but applies to moons as well.': Uses Tethys's elliptical orbit as the example and explicitly notes the law extends to moons — exactly what the notes provide for. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Kepler's first law...describes the orbits of Saturn and other planets...states that the object being orbited is one of the ellipse's foci.': Focuses on the foci detail rather than the elliptical shape, and provides no moon example. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Kepler's first law...for example, planetary satellites orbit their planets in an elliptical fashion.': Does not use the specific Tethys example from the notes and is vague about moons. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
